name: Alastor
description: Alastor, in Greek mythology, is a complex figure often associated with retribution, vengeance, and the avenging spirit. He is not a specific deity but rather a personification or type of daimon (spirit). Alastors are often described as spirits of vengeance who relentlessly pursue wrongdoers, particularly those who have committed heinous crimes such as murder within a family or betrayal of sacred oaths. They act as agents of divine justice, ensuring that transgressions are punished, often through generational curses or driving the guilty to madness and despair. The concept of the Alastor is tied to the ancient Greek belief in the importance of maintaining cosmic balance and the inevitable consequences of disrupting that balance through immoral acts. Their presence highlights the inescapable nature of justice and the long-lasting repercussions of unethical behavior. Alastors can be seen as manifestations of the collective guilt and moral outrage generated by particularly egregious offenses, embodying the inherent societal demand for retribution. The term 'Alastor' can also refer to an individual who is cursed or haunted by such a spirit, forever marked by the consequences of their actions or the actions of their ancestors, destined to suffer for the sins of the past.
subtitle: Spirit of Vengeance and Retribution
appearance: Alastor's appearance is not fixed, often manifesting as a shadowy figure, a haunting presence, or even an internal voice driving the guilty to despair. Some accounts depict him as a monstrous figure with glowing eyes and sharp claws, embodying the terror of divine retribution. Other times, he remains unseen, acting through subtle manipulations and psychological torment, reflecting the insidious nature of guilt and vengeance. The form Alastor takes is often tailored to the specific sins of the individual he is pursuing, amplifying their fears and insecurities.
habitat: Alastor is not bound to a specific location; he haunts the guilty, pursuing them relentlessly wherever they may go. He is most prevalent in places where heinous crimes have been committed, lingering in the atmosphere of guilt and despair. His presence is felt in the minds of the wrongdoers, driving them to madness and despair. He can also manifest in locations connected to the victims of injustice, serving as a constant reminder of the unresolved wrongs.
behavior: Alastor is driven by an insatiable thirst for vengeance and a commitment to restoring cosmic balance. He is relentless in his pursuit of justice, often employing cruel and unusual methods to punish the guilty. He delights in the suffering of wrongdoers, reveling in their despair and torment. He is cunning and manipulative, exploiting their weaknesses and fears to drive them to madness. He is a force of chaos and destruction, disrupting the lives of those he targets and leaving a trail of ruin in his wake.

keyMyths.title: The Curse of the Atreides
keyMyths.description: The House of Atreus was plagued by an Alastor due to the cycle of violence and betrayal that characterized their family history. Agamemnon's sacrifice of Iphigenia and Clytemnestra's subsequent murder of Agamemnon are examples of events that fueled the Alastor's power.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Provides early accounts of spirits and daimons in Greek cosmology.
sources.text: Provides early accounts of spirits and daimons in Greek cosmology.

sources.author: Aeschylus
sources.description: Illustrates the concept of Alastor through the curse on the House of Atreus.
sources.text: Illustrates the concept of Alastor through the curse on the House of Atreus.
